metadata.title: Accelerated Decline in Hepatic Glucose Production During Fasting in Normal Women Compared With Men
metadata.study_design: Comparative study of glucose kinetics in fasting men and women
metadata.demographics: Five normal men and five normal women in the midfollicular phase of menstrual cycle, matched for age and body mass index, with no history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease

evidence.quote: Accelerated Decline in Hepatic Glucose Production During Fasting in Normal Women Compared With Men
John N. Clore, Penny S. Glickman, Shirley T. Helm, John E. Nestler, and William G. Blackard
five normal men and five normal women underwent sequentiel S3H-glucose infusions after both a 14 and a 64hour fast

Five of the subjects were male and five were female in the midfollicular phase of their menstrual cycle. None had a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease or were on any medications. Subjects were matched for age and body mass index (BMI).
five normal men and five normal women underwent sequentiel S3H-glucose infusions

methods_participants.label: Participant sex and menstrual cycle phase
methods_participants.value: 5 male and 5 female in midfollicular phase

evidence.quote: Five of the subjects were male and five were female in the midfollicular phase of their menstrual cycle

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - health status
methods_participants.value: No history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, kidney disease, or medications

evidence.quote: None had a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease or were on any medications
Baseline blood samples were obtained at 7 AM (after 13 hours of fasting)
After completion of the first study (10 AM), the fast was continued for an additional 48 hours
A primed (25 µCi) continuous (0.25 µCi) infusion of 3-3H-glucose (New England Nuclear, Boston) was administered via a forearm vein from 7 AM until 10 AM
A primed (25 µCi) continuous (0.25 µCi) infusion of 3-3H-glucose
A primed (25 µCi) continuous (0.25 µCi) infusion of 3-3H-glucose

methods_participants.label: Blood sampling timing
methods_participants.value: 20-minute intervals during last hour of study

evidence.quote: blood samples were obtained at 20-minute intervals during the last hour of the study (9 AM to 10 AM)
Body composition was assessed by skinfold measurements and impedance plethysmography (BIA-101 Body Composition Analyzer, RJL Systems, Detroit)
Plasma glucose was measured immediately after centrifugation by the glucose oxidase method (Beckman, Fullerton, CA)
Plasma insulin, C-peptide, growth hormone, and glucagon were determined using double-antibody radioimmunoassays
Cortisol was measured by direct radioimmunoassay (Gammacoat assay, Clinical Assays, Cambridge, MA)
Plasma FFA was determined by enzymatic methods

narrative_results.statement: Plasma glucose values fell by 24% from the 16th to the 64th hour of fasting in both men and women

evidence.quote: Plasma glucose values fell by 24% from the 16th to the 64th hour of fasting in both men (5.23 + 0.03 v 3.96 + 0.14 mmol/L, P < .OOl) and women (4.84 + 0.14 v 3.65 f 0.25 mmol/L, P < .OOl).

narrative_results.statement: Rates of glucose appearance (Ra) fell significantly in both men and women during the 64-hour fast

evidence.quote: Rates of glucose appearance (Ra) fell from 2.11 + 0.24 to 1.79 * 0.24 mg.kg-'.min-' (PC .Ol), and 2.22 f 0.17 to 1.67 + 0.12 mg.kg-'.min-' (P -c .Ol) in men and women, respectively

narrative_results.statement: The fall in Ra was significantly greater in women compared with men

evidence.quote: Both the absolute (0.55 + 0.06 v 0.32 * 0.06 mg.kg-'.min-') and the percent fall in Ra (24.6% + 1.4% v 15.8% t 3.0%, Fig 1, inset) were significantly greater in women compared with men (P -e .05).

narrative_results.statement: Plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast

evidence.quote: plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast (P < .05).

narrative_results.statement: Changes in plasma glucose were associated with decreases in plasma insulin and C-peptide as well as increases in plasma glucagon

evidence.quote: Changes in plasma glucose were associated with decreases in plasma insulin and C-peptide as well as increases in plasma glucagon (Table 2).

narrative_results.statement: All subjects exhibited at least a sixfold increase in β-hydroxybutyrate levels during the fast

evidence.quote: all subjects exhibited at least a sixfold increase during the fast.

narrative_results.statement: 64-hour fasted women exhibit a greater fall in HGO than do men similarly fasted

evidence.quote: In conclusion, 64-hour fasted women exhibit a greater fall in HGO than do men similarly fasted.

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 states fasting duration of 14 hours for first study; Pass 2 only mentions glucose infusion duration of 3 hours without addressing fasting. These describe different parameters and cannot be directly reconciled.
Pass 1 lists two methods (skinfold AND impedance plethysmography), while Pass 2 lists only skinfold measurements. Incompatible scope of methods.
Pass 1 describes hormone associations with glucose changes; Pass 2 describes alanine concentration changes. These are distinct facts about different metabolites

semantic_groups.rationale: Pass 1 specifies both sexes and cycle phase; Pass 2 only mentions female cycle phase. Both confirm midfollicular phase for females, but Pass 1 provides additional participant composition detail.
Pass 1 lists multiple exclusion criteria including diabetes; Pass 2 focuses only on diabetes history. Both confirm no diabetes mellitus history, but Pass 1 is more comprehensive.

Pass 1 states fasting duration of 14 hours for first study; Pass 2 only mentions glucose infusion duration of 3 hours without addressing fasting. These describe different parameters and cannot be directly reconciled.
Both confirm 64-hour fasting duration for second study. Minor wording variation in labeling.
Both describe primed continuous infusion of 3-3H-glucose as the glucose tracer infusion method. Pass 2 adds 'isotope tracer' descriptor but conveys the same fact.
Pass 1 provides primed glucose tracer dose (25 µCi); Pass 2 is empty. Information only in Pass 1.
Pass 1 provides continuous glucose tracer dose (0.25 µCi); Pass 2 is empty. Information only in Pass 1.
Both confirm blood sampling at 20-minute intervals. Pass 1 adds temporal context (during last hour) but core fact is the same.
Pass 1 lists two methods (skinfold AND impedance plethysmography), while Pass 2 lists only skinfold measurements. Incompatible scope of methods.
Both describe glucose oxidase method for plasma glucose measurement. Pass 1 includes instrument brand (Beckman) while Pass 2 omits it. Same underlying fact.
Both describe double-antibody radioimmunoassay for insulin measurement. Negligible wording differences.
Both describe direct radioimmunoassay for cortisol. Pass 1 includes specific assay name (Gammacoat) while Pass 2 generalizes to radioimmunoassay. Same underlying method.
Pass 1 contains free fatty acid measurement method information; Pass 2 is empty. Orphan entry from Pass 1.
Pass 1 contains mean age for men; Pass 2 is empty. Orphan entry from Pass 1.
Pass 1 contains mean age for women; Pass 2 is empty. Orphan entry from Pass 1.
Pass 1 contains mean weight for men; Pass 2 is empty. Orphan entry from Pass 1.
Pass 1 contains mean weight for women; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains mean BMI for men; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains mean BMI for women; Pass 2 is empty
Both describe male participant count of 5; Pass 1 presents combined information while Pass 2 isolates male count
Pass 2 contains female participant count of 5; Pass 1 is empty
Pass 2 contains fasting duration information; Pass 1 is empty
Pass 2 contains inclusion criteria regarding hypertension; Pass 1 is empty
Pass 2 contains inclusion criteria regarding kidney disease; Pass 1 is empty
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains information about medication inclusion criteria
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains information about glucagon assay method
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains information about body composition assessment method
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains information about fasting compliance assessment
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains information about catheter placement
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains information about saline infusion concentration
Identical statements about glucose appearance rates during fasting
Identical statements about sex differences in Ra decline
Identical statements about plasma glycerol sex differences
Pass 1 describes hormone associations with glucose changes; Pass 2 describes alanine concentration changes. These are distinct facts about different metabolites
Same fact about β-hydroxybutyrate increase; only difference is spelling (β-hydroxybutyrate vs beta-hydroxybutyrate)
Identical statements about HGO sex differences during fasting
Identical statements about plasma glucose decline with specific percentage
Pass 2 contains a statement about glucose clearance rates with no corresponding extraction in Pass 1
